result.markdown:
--- title: "How long should a block be?" author: "Léo Belzile, HEC Montréal
(joint work with Anthony Davison)" subtitle: "One World Extremes" date: "2026-03-26" date-format: "dddd, MMM D, YYYY" eval: true echo: false cache: true standalone: true bibliography: BlockSize.bib format: revealjs: slide-number: true html-math-method: mathjax preview-links: auto theme: [simple, hecmontreal.scss] title-slide-attributes: data-background-color: "#002855" logo: "figures/logo_hec_montreal_bleu_web.png" revealjs-plugins: - revealjs-text-resizer filters: - highlight-text --- ::: {.cell} ::: ## Block maximum method Idea promoted for engineering applications by Emil Gumbel [cf. @Gumbel:1958]. - divide a (time) series of length $b$ into $n_b$ equal-length disjoint blocks of $b$ successive observations, - fit the generalized extreme value (GEV) distribution to the resulting sample of $n_b$ block maxima, - use the fitted model for risk assessment. ## Motivating example ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Daily maximum wind gust speed in Cheeseboro, California, for the months of January (plus Feb. 1st) 1996--2026, for a total of $32 \times 31$ measurements.](Block_size-presentation_files/figure-revealjs/fig-cheeseboro-1.png){#fig-cheeseboro fig-align='center' width=80%} ::: ::: ## Extremal types theorem The justification for using the GEV comes from @Fisher.Tippett:1928, @Gnedenko:1943, @vonMises:1954. If $G$ is a distribution function and there exist location and scaling sequences $d_m$ and $c_m>0$ such that the maximum of $m$ observations has a non-degenerate limiting distribution $$ \lim_{m \to \infty}G^m(c_mx+d_m) = F(x) $$ then $F(x)$ must be a GEV distribution function. ## Generalized extreme value distribution The GEV distribution function is \begin{align*} F(x; \mu, \sigma, \xi)=\begin{cases} \exp\left[-\left\{1+\xi \frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right\}_{+}^{-1/\xi}\right], & \xi \neq 0, \\ \exp\left[-\exp\left\{-\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right\}\right], & \xi =0. \end{cases} \end{align*} with location $\mu\in \mathbb{R},$ scale $\sigma>0$ and shape $\xi\in \mathbb{R}.$ The support of $\mathsf{GEV}(\mu, \sigma, \xi)$ is $\{x: \sigma + \xi(x-\mu) > 0\}.$ ## Max-stability The GEV is characterized by *max-stability*: if $X_{j} \stackrel{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim} \mathsf{GEV}(\mu, \sigma, \xi)$, then for any $m,$ \begin{align*} Y = \max\{X_{1}, \ldots, X_{m}\} \sim \mathsf{GEV}(\mu_m, \sigma_m, \xi), \end{align*} with \begin{align*} \mu_m &=\mu + \sigma (m^\xi-1)/\xi, &\sigma_m &= \sigma m^\xi, &\text{if }\xi &\neq 0,\\ \mu_m &= \mu+\sigma\log m, & \sigma_m &=\sigma,&\text{if }\xi &= 0. \end{align*} ## Stationary setting The extremal types theorem extends to stationary time series [@Leadbetter:1974;@Leadbetter.Lindgren.Rootzen:1983;@Leadbetter:1983]. Under a condition $D(u_n)$ and for extremal index $\theta \in (0,1],$ we get instead \begin{align*} \lim_{m \to \infty} G^{\theta m}(c_{m}x+d_{m})&= F(x) \end{align*} or equivalently \begin{align*} \lim_{m \to \infty} G^{m}(c_{\theta m}x+d_{\theta m}) &= F(x). \end{align*} ## Research question: How to choose the block size $m$? Traditional extreme value bias-variance trade-off: - Smaller block sizes may lead to poor GEV approximation (potentially biased extrapolation). - Larger blocks lead to smaller sample sizes (more variability). ## Other considerations - Data availability (frequency of records). - Block maximum is widely viewed as more robust approach in the applied literature. - Theoretical consideration of asymptotic efficiency relative to peaks-over-threshold [@Bucher.Zhou:2021]. - Natural (minimum) block size (e.g., annual for seasonally-varying data). ## Literature review Surprisingly, very little work in the area. - @Wang:2016 suggest using combinations of goodness-of-fit tests (such as Anderson--Darling or Kolmogorov--Smirnov). - unpublished work of @Dkengne:2020 suggests parameter stability plot built using max-stability. ## Diagnostic plots ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Gumbel plot of $m$-day maxima against standard Gumbel plotting positions $-\log[-\log\{i/(n+1)\}]$ for the Cheeseboro wind gust data.](Block_size-presentation_files/figure-revealjs/fig-gumbelplot-1.png){#fig-gumbelplot fig-align='center' width=70%} ::: ::: ::: {style="font-size: 65%;"} Plot *à la* @Cox.Isham.Northrop:2002. With Gumbel fit, lines should be parallel, but we see some evidence of distortion for $b=1$ or $b=2$ days. ::: ## Testing for different block sizes ::: {style="font-size: 85%;"} Consider blocks of size $b$ versus $bm$ for $b, m \in \mathbb{N}.$ We gather observations into an $n_b/m \times m$ matrix of ordered inputs. ::: ::: {#tbl-gust-block .cell tbl-cap='Blocks of size $m=4$ from daily maxima wind gusts (in km/h).'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
23 42 105 108
14 23 29 61
18 32 48 79
21 26 31 66
32 43 45 77
``` ::: ::: ::: {style="font-size: 65%;"} Row $i$ contains the order statistics $X_{i, (1)} \leq \cdots \leq X_{i, (m)}.$ ::: ## Joint distribution of order statistics Let $X_{(1)} \leq \cdots \le X_{(m)}$ denote the order statistics. Let $F$ and $f$ be the CDF and density of $X_j \stackrel{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}\mathsf{GEV}(\mu, \sigma, \xi).$ The joint density of order statistics is the product of - the conditional $X_{(1)}, \ldots, X_{(m-1)} \mid X_{(m)} = x_{(m)},$ - the marginal $X_{(m)}$. ## Conditional density of lower order statistics The conditional density of $X_{(1)}, \ldots, X_{(m-1)} \mid X_{(m)}$ is \begin{align*} f_{{(1, \ldots, m-1)} \mid {(m)}}(\boldsymbol{x}_{-(m)}) &= (m-1)!\prod_{j=1}^{m-1}\frac{f(x_{(j)}; \mu, \sigma, \xi)}{F(x_{(m)}; \mu, \sigma, \xi)} \end{align*} so the $(m-1)$ smallest observations are, up to permutation, $\mathsf{GEV}(\mu, \sigma, \xi)$ truncated above at $x_{(m)}$. ## Distribution of block maximum The block maximum $Y=\max(X_1,\ldots, X_m)$ has CDF $F_{(m)}(x)=F^m(x)$ and density \begin{align*} f_{(m)}(x) &= m F(x; \mu, \sigma, \xi)^{m-1}f(x; \mu, \sigma, \xi) \\ &= f(x; \mu_m, \sigma_m, \xi), \end{align*} by max-stability. ## Likelihood ::: {style="font-size: 90%;"} We write \begin{align*} \mathcal{L}({\boldsymbol{\vartheta}}_0, {\boldsymbol{\vartheta}}_m)=\prod_{i=1}^n f_{{(1, \ldots, m-1)} \mid {(m)}}(\boldsymbol{x}_{i, -(m)};{\boldsymbol{\vartheta}}_0)f_{(m)}(x_{i,(m)}; {\boldsymbol{\vartheta}}_m), \end{align*} where - ${\boldsymbol{\vartheta}}_0 = (\mu, \sigma, \xi)$ are the parameters for $X_{i, (1)} \leq \cdots \le X_{i, (m-1)}.$ - ${\boldsymbol{\vartheta}}_m$ are the parameters of the largest observation $X_{i, (m)}.$ Under the null hypothesis of max-stability, ${\boldsymbol{\vartheta}}_m=(\mu_m, \sigma_m, \xi) = g(\boldsymbol{\vartheta}_0),$ say. ::: ## Alternative hypotheses The idea underlying our testing procedure is to let the parameters of the block maximum differ, by ::: {style="font-size: 80%;"} - $A_1$: setting $\mathcal{H}_a: {\boldsymbol{\vartheta}}_m = (\mu_{\omega m}, \sigma_{\omega m}, \xi)$ for some scaling $\omega > 0$, and testing $\mathcal{H}_0: \omega=1.$ - $A_2$: allowing both location and scale to vary $\mathcal{H}_a: {\boldsymbol{\vartheta}}_m = (\mu_m+\nu, \phi \sigma_m, \xi)$ and testing $\mathcal{H}_0: \nu=0, \phi=1.$ - $A_3$: letting all three parameters vary $\mathcal{H}_a: {\boldsymbol{\vartheta}}_m = (\mu_m+\nu, \phi \sigma_m, \xi + \zeta)$ and testing $\mathcal{H}_0:\nu=0, \phi=1, \zeta=0.$ We use likelihood ratio statistics to compare the models $\mathcal{H}_0$ and $\mathcal{H}_a.$ The asymptotic distribution of the test is $\chi^2_k$ with $k\in\{1, 2, 3\}.$ ::: ## Power study stup We consider different scenarios to assess the power of tests: - sample sizes $n \in \{25,50,100\},$ - number of replicates $m\in \{2, 5, 10\},$ - three alternatives $\mathcal{H}_a.$ All power curves are based on 2000 replications, with tests conducted at level 5%. ## Power study We consider maxima of (a) $\mathsf{normal}(0,1)$ and (b) $\mathsf{Weibull}(1, 0.8).$ with distribution $G$ and block of size $30,$ simulate - $X_{(1)}, \ldots, X_{(m-1)}$ from $G^{30},$ - $X_{(m)}$ from $G^{30\delta},$ but left-truncated at $x_{(m-1)}$. ## Power study - generalized extreme value data {.smaller} We use the penultimate approximation to get GEV parameters and simulate from these. Both have limiting shape $\xi=0,$ but convergence is slow. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![GEV parameters based on max-stable (dashed), and the penultimate approximation (full) for Gaussian (top) and Weibull (bottom).](Block_size-presentation_files/figure-revealjs/fig-penultnorm-1.png){#fig-penultnorm width=100%} ::: ::: ## Power curves - GEV ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](Block_size-presentation_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-3-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: ## Power study - maximum domain of attraction ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](Block_size-presentation_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-4-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: ::: {style="font-size: 60%;"} We simulate directly block maxima from $G^{30},$ where $G$ is the Gaussian or Weibull CDF, but where the largest observation is drawn from $G^{30\delta}.$ ::: ## Summary of power study - As expected, power increases with the sample size $n$. - Perhaps surprisingly, the test has more power when $m$ is small! - Alternative 1 is always the more powerful (less degrees of freedom), despite misspecification. - There is a small size distortion (about 5%) for alternative 3 in small samples. ## Stationary sequences with extremal clustering {.smaller} To investigate serial dependence, we also consider data from first-order max-autoregressive (MAR) processes parametrized in terms of the extremal index $\theta.$ Consider the stochastic process obtained through the recursion [@Tavares:1977] \begin{align*} X_i &= \max\{X_{i-1}, Z_i\} +\log(1 - \theta), \quad i \geq 1 \\ Z_i &\sim \mathsf{GEV}\{\log(\theta) - \log(1-\theta),1,0\} \end{align*} The margins are standard Gumbel, $X_i \sim \mathsf{GEV}(0,1,0),$ while the distribution of $Y = \max\{X_1, \ldots, X_{m}\}$ is Gumbel with location $\log[(m-1)\theta+1].$ ## Location parameters of stationary sequences {.smaller} Max-stability holds asymptotically, but with $m\theta$ rather than $m$. ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![MAR with $\xi=0$ and $\theta=0.5$: time series of simulated values (left) and location parameters of the Gumbel maxima based on extrapolating to blocks of size $m$, taking $\log(m)$ (iid) and $\log(m\theta)$ (stationary) as location parameters.](Block_size-presentation_files/figure-revealjs/fig-tavares77-1.png){#fig-tavares77 fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: ## Max-autoregressive process with positive shape ::: {style="font-size: 80%;"} For $\xi>0$, the first-order max-autoregressive model is \begin{align*} X_i &= \max\{(1-\theta)^\xi X_{i-1}, Z_i\}, \qquad i \geq 1 \\ Z_i &\sim \mathsf{GEV}(\theta^{\xi}, \xi\theta^{\xi}, \xi) \end{align*} The marginal stationary distribution is Fréchet with shape $\xi^{-1}$, i.e., a $\mathsf{GEV}(1, \xi, \xi).$ The distribution of $Y= \max\{X_1, \ldots, X_{m}\}$ has CDF $\exp[-\{\theta(m-1)+1\}x^{-1/\xi}];$ see Example 10.3 of @Beirlant:2004. ::: ## Power curves - max autoregressive {.smaller} ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](Block_size-presentation_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-5-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: ## Power study - doubling the block size with MAR ::: {style="font-size: 60%;"} As the empirical block size gets larger, the GEV approximation should become effective and directly incorporate the impact of $\theta$. ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](Block_size-presentation_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-6-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: ## Comments on power curves - More discrepancies are expected for smaller values of $\theta,$ as the average cluster size is $1/\theta,$ the reciprocal of the extremal index. - As the block size increases, the effect of $\theta$ is captured through parameter estimates and we fail to reject for larger $\theta,$ as expected. - Alternative 3 suffers again from size distortion, especially in small samples (either when $m$ or $n$ is small). ## Diagnostic plots We propose quantile-quantile plots to assess the goodness-of-fit of the model. Among quantities of interest - all maxima, - the block maximum $X_{(m)}$, - the spacing $X_{(m)} - X_{(m-1)}$. We use a parametric bootstrap scheme for uncertainty quantification. ## Pivots Under the null hypothesis, the $F(X_{j}; \boldsymbol{\vartheta})$ are uniform and standard results on order statistics of uniform samples gives for the spacing \begin{align*} S(\boldsymbol{\vartheta}) = F(X_{(m)}; \boldsymbol{\vartheta})- F(X_{(m-1)}; \boldsymbol{\vartheta}) &\sim \mathsf{beta}(1, m) \end{align*} and for the largest observation \begin{align*} F(X_{(m)}; \boldsymbol{\vartheta}) &\sim \mathsf{beta}(m, 1). \end{align*} ::: {style="font-size: 60%;"} Alternative pivots can be obtained, e.g., via Rényi's representation. ::: ## Parametric bootstrap {.smaller} A conventional parametric bootstrap to test properties of the statistic $S(\boldsymbol{\vartheta})$ would - simulate uniform draws $U_{(1)}^* \le \cdots \le U_{(m)}^*.$ - map them to GEV via the quantile function $X_j^*=F^{-1}(U_i^*; \widehat{\boldsymbol{\vartheta}})$ using the MLE $\widehat{\boldsymbol{\vartheta}}$, letting $X^*_{j} \sim \mathsf{GEV}(\widehat{\mu}, \widehat{\sigma}, \widehat{\xi}).$ - obtain maximum likelihood estimates $\widehat{\boldsymbol{\vartheta}}^{*}$ from $\boldsymbol{X}^*.$ - use the distribution of the back-transformed values of $X^{*}_{(m)}$ and $X^{*}_{(m-1)}$, e.g., $S(\boldsymbol{X}{}^*; \widehat{\boldsymbol{\vartheta}}{}^{*})$ - apply the CDF of the beta distribution to get uniform draws. ## Distortion due to plug-in The null distribution of $S(\widehat{\boldsymbol{\vartheta}})$ depends on the unknown true parameter value $\boldsymbol{\vartheta}$, and although this is replaced by an estimator that is consistent under the null hypothesis, some distortion remains. The resulting error can be reduced by nested bootstrapping [@Davison.Hinkley:1997, section 3.9], but this may be too computationally complex for routine use. ## Marginal likelihood Since interest lies in extrapolation, we can also consider estimation of GEV parameters based on the marginal distribution of $X_{(1)}, \ldots, X_{(m-1)}$, where \begin{align*} \mathcal{L}_{\text{marg}}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) &= \prod_{i=1}^n f_{(1),\ldots, (m-1)}(\boldsymbol{x}_i; \boldsymbol{\theta})\\&\propto \prod_{i=1}^n\{1-F(x_{i,(m-1)})\}\prod_{j=1}^{m-1} f(x_{i,(j)};\boldsymbol{\theta}) \end{align*} which amounts to right-censoring the largest observation. ## (Dis)advantages of the marginal likelihood {.smaller} - Avoids using the maximum both for model estimation and goodness-of-fit: no double dipping. - Must also include the inequality constraint $\sigma_0+\xi_0 (\max_{i} x_{i,(m)}-\mu_0) > 0$ on the optimization. ::: {.column width="50%"} - Discarding the largest observation leads to a loss of information: with $m=4$, we calculate the efficiency for $\boldsymbol{\vartheta}=(\mu=0, \sigma=1, \xi),$$$r(\boldsymbol{\vartheta})=\frac{ |\imath_{\text{marg}}(\boldsymbol{\vartheta})|^{1/3}}{|\imath_{\text{full}}(\boldsymbol{\vartheta})|^{1/3}},$$ where $\imath$ is the Fisher information. ::: :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.cell layout-align="right"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](Block_size-presentation_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-7-1.png){fig-align='right' width=90%} ::: ::: ::: :::: ## P-values for test for wind gusts at Cheeseboro ::: {.cell} ::: ::: {#tbl-pvals-cheeseboro .cell tbl-cap='P-values (rounded to three digits) for the test of max-stability.'} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
\(m=2\) \(m=4\)
block \(A_1\) \(A_2\) \(A_3\) \(A_1\) \(A_2\) \(A_3\)
\(b=1\) 0 0 0 0 0 0
\(b=2\) 0.005 0.02 0 0.081 0.21 0
\(b=4\) 0.279 0.47 0.16 0.252 0.47 0.17
\(b=8\) 0.176 0.38 0.47 0.743 0.76 0.82
``` ::: ::: ## Probability-probability plots of maxima ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](Block_size-presentation_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-9-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ::: {style="font-size: 70%;"} We obtain pointwise and simultaneous confidence intervals using the envelope method [section 4.2.4 of @Davison.Hinkley:1997]. ::: ## Quantile-quantile plot of all maxima ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](Block_size-presentation_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-10-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ::: {style="font-size: 70%;"} Work in progress to account for rounding. ::: ## Risk extrapolation ::: {style="font-size: 65%;"} The block size can have a sizeable impact on risk estimation (sample maximum is 148km/h). Shape of $\widehat{\xi}=0.18$ for $b=1,$ versus $\xi=-0.09$ for $b=4.$ ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Density of the 50-year maximum for the Cheeseboro wind gust data, based on different block size and maximum likelihood estimates.](Block_size-presentation_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-11-1.png){fig-align='center' width=960} ::: ::: ## Inference for extremes Use GEV model fitted to blocks of size $4$ days to calculate risk measures for the maximum 50 years wind gust, by using the max-stability relationship with $m=8 \times 50.$ - The estimated median of half-century maximum daily wind gust is 139.6 km/h - 95% profile-based confidence intervals: (125.9km/h, 168.5km/h). - The serial correlation is negligible. ## Summary {.smaller} We propose likelihood ratio tests for testing max-stability for $n_b$ blocks of size $b$ - Build blocks of $n_b/m \times m$ ordered data. - Check fit to largest (max of $m$) and use max-stability to detect departures. - Simulation studies show the test is powerful even in small samples. - The test with a single parameter seems more powerful than competitors, more for $m=2.$ Diagnostic plots based on bootstrap to account for goodness-of-fit. - Different values give different indications about the fit (all observations, only the max of blocks of size $bm$, spacings, etc.) give visual indications of goodness-of-fit. ## Limitations {.smaller} - We consider only $bm$ versus $b$: - does not allow for comparison of blocks of size 2 vs 3, say. - some data discarded if $n$ is not divisible by $bm$ (but could use ideas from @Simpson.Northrop:2026). - in practice it is natural to consider aggregation of periods, e.g., monthly to yearly. - The scheme leads to multiple testing if multiple values of $m$ and block sizes are considered: - smaller values of $m$ should lead to higher power, as shown through simulations, - but the choice of $m$ should be dictated more by practical considerations. - Our approach is restricted to independent or stationary time series. ## Extensions and future work ::: {style="font-size: 90%;"} - Accounting for rounding (work in progress) and more guidelines for the choice of graphical diagnostic. - Combining model with estimates of the extremal index $\theta.$ - Alternative estimators for the GEV built from sliding blocks [e.g., @Bucher.Zanger:2023]. Theory for these is more complicated, owing to lack of genuine likelihood. - Could consider Wald-based tests with sandwich-based or bootstrap covariance estimators. ::: ## EVA 2027 - Montréal, July 5th-9th, 2027. ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](figures/Logo_EVA2027e.svg){fig-align='center' width=50%} ::: ::: Website: [`https://hecsciencesdecision.github.io/eva2027/`](https://hecsciencesdecision.github.io/eva2027/) ## References

include-after-body:
<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
